steps.title: Identify the sampling method and its significance
steps.content: The organizer selected 55 teams at random from all trivia teams in the tournament. Random selection from a population is the key principle that determines generalizability: a random sample is representative of the population it was drawn from.

steps.title: Recognize what the table represents
steps.content: The table displays results for only 40 of the 55 teams—specifically those that practiced at least 3 hours per week. This is a subset of the original random sample, but it remains part of that random sample.

steps.title: Apply the generalization principle
steps.content: Because the original 55 teams were randomly selected from the tournament, results based on any subset of those 55 teams can be generalized to the tournament population. The random selection ensures the sample (and its subsets) are representative of the tournament, regardless of the restriction shown in the table.

steps.title: Evaluate each choice
steps.content:
Choice A: Restricts to teams scoring 14+ points — the study's sampling method (random selection) wasn't designed to target only high-scoring teams.

Choice B: Only includes the 55 teams, not the tournament — the sample of 55 came FROM the tournament, not the other way around.

Choice C: Only the 40 teams in the table — this is too narrow; the table is a filtered view of the larger random sample.

Choice D: All tournament teams — the original random selection of 55 teams from the tournament ensures results can be generalized to the entire tournament population. That matches choice D — D is correct.
